metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py` (with `--replay`, `--assert-blocks`, `--assert-false-blocks` flags) is committed as part of sibling story 9f25 before this story is executed, or update AC4 to reference the actual existing script (`calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py`) and its real CLI interface.
findings.finding: The plan's AC4 proving command references `python docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py --replay --corpus .tickets-tracker --threshold <derived> --assert-blocks 518-class,539-class --assert-false-blocks 0` as an existing deliverable from sibling story 9f25, but no such file exists anywhere in the repo — `docs/experiments/` contains only `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` and `ab_impact_model.py`, and a full-repo search finds no `derive_code_review_threshold.py`. The plan treats the script as already-available ('the replay mode of 9f25's derivation script'), creating a false premise that the AC4 verification step is executable.
findings.location: AC4 / Scope ('Depends on the derivation story 9f25 (consumes its derived threshold + its code-review derivation/replay script)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm with 9f25 that `derive_code_review_threshold.py` will expose `--replay`, `--assert-blocks`, and `--assert-false-blocks` flags as AC4 assumes, or update AC4's proving command to match the interface 9f25 actually ships.
findings.finding: AC4's proving command references specific CLI flags (--replay, --assert-blocks 518-class,539-class, --assert-false-blocks 0) on docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py, but this script does not yet exist in the repo (ticket 9f25 is still 'open' and lists the script as a future file_impact artifact). Neither 9f25's description nor its ACs specify these flags, so the plan asserts a capability of a not-yet-written tool.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4 — proving command: `python docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py --replay --corpus .tickets-tracker --threshold <derived> --assert-blocks 518-class,539-class --assert-false-blocks 0`
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit note in the Scope or Dependencies section that 9f25's script MUST expose `--replay --assert-blocks <ids> --assert-false-blocks <n>` flags, and either add this to 9f25's AC or document the interface contract here so both stories align.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC4's proving command to make it explicitly conditional on 9f25 being closed and its script committed (e.g. add a precondition note: 'Requires 9f25 delivered; skip if `derive_code_review_threshold.py` absent'), or add a hard dependency gate that blocks this story from merging until 9f25 is closed.
findings.finding: AC4's proving command references `docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py` as the replay script, but this file does not exist in the repo — it is a deliverable of the sibling ticket 9f25 (currently open, undelivered). The AC is written as an unconditional executable check with a concrete command line, yet that command cannot run until 9f25 closes and commits the script.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4 — proving command: `python docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py --replay ...`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Coordinate with 9f25 to define (and commit to) the exact CLI interface of `derive_code_review_threshold.py` — specifically the `--replay`, `--corpus`, `--threshold`, `--assert-blocks`, and `--assert-false-blocks` flags — OR update AC4's proving command to match whatever interface 9f25 actually ships.
findings.finding: AC4's proving command specifies five CLI flags (`--replay`, `--corpus`, `--threshold`, `--assert-blocks`, `--assert-false-blocks`) on `docs/experiments/derive_code_review_threshold.py` — a script that does not yet exist and is 9f25's deliverable. 9f25's own ACs and description commit only to 'commit a dedicated code-review derivation script' with no mention of a `--replay` mode or these specific flags. The interface is Unverified: if 9f25 ships the script with a different CLI surface (e.g. no `--replay` flag, or `--expected-blocks` instead of `--assert-blocks`), AC4's proving command will fail at execution time.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (proving command)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 9f25's AC: 'The derivation script exposes a `--replay --corpus <path> --threshold <t> --assert-blocks <ids> --assert-false-blocks <n>` interface usable as the AC4 proving command in the sibling enablement story.' Then AC4 can reference that committed interface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define '518-class' and '539-class' in the plan (e.g., 'findings labeled `security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere` with score ≥0.6 in the post-recal corpus sidecars') and confirm the derive script's --assert-blocks flag matches this definition.
findings.finding: The replay proving command in AC4 uses the notation '518-class,539-class' without defining what makes a finding a member of these classes, making the assertion an underdetermined fixture (defect type 4: fixture invalidity).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4, proving command flag `--assert-blocks 518-class,539-class`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision` criterion ownership ambiguity before implementation: specify a concrete lookup procedure (e.g., query the post-recal corpus for findings where this binary is `true` and tally their `criteria` values), and pin the result in the mapping table rather than leaving it 'to be confirmed against the verifier prompts'.
findings.finding: The `silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision` → `scope-intent` (or `security`) mapping is explicitly unresolved in the plan ('to be confirmed against the verifier prompts during implementation'), but no concrete confirmation procedure is specified. The `scope-intent` overlay is designed to detect out-of-scope diff hunks — findings tagged `criteria: ['scope-intent']`. The verifier binary `silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision` describes a silently-wrong value flowing into a real decision, which is semantically a security/correctness concern, matching the `security` overlay's prompt ('taint flow to a dangerous sink', 'fail-open error handling'). If this binary is predominantly set on findings tagged `criteria: ['security']` rather than `criteria: ['scope-intent']`, then enabling `scope-intent` as blocking provides no protection for this serious consequence, and the plan would fail to block the intended finding class without enabling `security`. Enabling `security` as blocking has higher coverage but potentially different false-block risk than 9f25's corpus analysis targeted.
findings.location: What section: '`silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision` → **scope-intent** (or **security** if its prompt owns it)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-implementation step: run `grep -r 'silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision.*true' .tickets-tracker/` on the sidecar corpus to tally the `criteria` field on matching findings, then hard-code the owning criterion in the mapping table. If both `security` and `scope-intent` appear, list both explicitly rather than `(or ...)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm which Pass-1 overlay prompt (base reviewer, `security`, or a dedicated correctness overlay) actually produces findings that set `silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision: true`, and update the binary→criterion mapping table to name that criterion — or add the corresponding recall item to the scope-intent prompt if scope-intent is the intended owner.
findings.finding: The plan maps `silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision` (a PRODUCTION-lane binary about a silently-wrong value feeding a real decision) to the `scope-intent` criterion, but the scope-intent overlay prompt exclusively detects diff hunks that fall outside ticket scope/AC — it has no rubric, recall list, or mechanism to produce a finding that would cause the Pass-2 verifier to set `silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision: true`. Enabling blocking on `scope-intent` for this binary would block zero instances of the intended defect type.
findings.location: What section — binary→criterion mapping table: `silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision` → **scope-intent**
findings.suggested_fix: Audit the base code-review reviewer prompt and the security overlay prompt for correctness/silent-wrong patterns; assign `silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision` to whichever criterion's recall rubric covers it (likely `security` or the base reviewer). If scope-intent is intended to own it, add a corresponding recall item to `code-review-scope-intent.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Finalize the binary→criterion mapping table before implementation begins (resolve 'scope-intent or security' for `silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision`, and remove the 'to be confirmed' hedge by stating the authoritative resolution method or the resolved mapping directly in the plan).
findings.finding: The binary-to-criterion mapping is explicitly marked 'to be confirmed against the verifier prompts during implementation', and `silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision` routes to 'scope-intent (or security if its prompt owns it)' — both are 'figure out' placeholders that defer a real design decision to the executor, violating E2's no-placeholder rule.
findings.location: ## What — mapping table and parenthetical '(or security if its prompt owns it)'
findings.suggested_fix: Run `grep -r 'silent_wrong_feeding' src/rebar/llm/code_review/` against the prompts now and record the owning criterion key in the plan as a fixed decision, e.g. '→ scope-intent (verified against prompts/scope_intent_v2.txt, commit abc123)'. If it cannot be determined pre-implementation, add an explicit sub-step 'Step 0: grep each binary name against verifier prompt files and record the owning routing key' with a concrete grep pattern and a verification artifact (a committed mapping comment in criteria_routing.json).

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or fold into an existing AC) requiring docs/plan-review-gate.md to be updated and verified, e.g. with a grep for the new blocking posture language.
findings.finding: docs/plan-review-gate.md ('the cross-gate posture note') is listed in Scope but has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion, leaving the update unverifiable and easy to omit.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'docs/plan-review-gate.md (the cross-gate posture note)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the provisional-vs-final contradiction: either (a) finalize the mapping table in the plan body and remove the 'to be confirmed' qualifier, or (b) add a sub-step to AC1 that requires the implementer to document the prompt-confirmed mapping before marking AC1 done.
findings.finding: The mapping table (serious consequence-binary → criteria routing key) is described as provisional in the What section ('to be confirmed against the verifier prompts during implementation') but AC1 treats it as finalized ('The approved serious-tier CRITERIA (routing keys per the mapping table above)'). An implementer could read AC1 as passing if they use the table as-is, while What implies the table may change — a cross-section contradiction about whether the table is settled.
findings.location: What section (mapping table + 'to be confirmed' note) vs AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-step: 'AC1a: Before flipping blocking_enabled, the implementer records in a PR comment the prompt-confirmed criterion↔binary mapping (deviations from the plan table must be noted). AC1 is not complete until AC1a is merged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Qualify the ADR 0036 edit target with the full filename: `docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md` (not the bare `0036-*.md` glob, since two files share that prefix).
findings.finding: The plan says 'Update ADR 0036' but there are two files with the `0036-` prefix: `docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md` (the correct target — it describes the permissive rollout invariant being concluded) and `docs/adr/0036-acli-429-rate-limit-backoff.md` (an unrelated backoff ADR). The plan does not supply the full filename, leaving an executor to guess which file to update.
findings.location: What section / Scope section — 'docs/adr/0036-*.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `docs/adr/0036-*.md` in Scope with `docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC3, e.g. `grep -E '9f25|derivation' docs/adr/0036-*.md` to confirm the dated enablement note is present.
findings.finding: AC3 ('ADR 0036 carries a dated enablement note citing derivation ticket 9f25') has no proving command — an executor cannot verify completion without manually reading the file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3
findings.suggested_fix: grep -E '(9f25|derivation.*threshold)' docs/adr/0036-*.md && echo 'ADR note present'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or fold into AC3) requiring that docs/plan-review-gate.md is updated to reflect the new blocking posture, with a stated verification step (e.g., the relevant paragraph references the new enabled criteria set).
findings.finding: The Scope section lists `docs/plan-review-gate.md (the cross-gate posture note)` as a file to be changed, but no Acceptance Criterion covers this deliverable — it is an orphan described deliverable with no verification step.
findings.location: Scope section ('docs/plan-review-gate.md') vs all ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'AC3b: docs/plan-review-gate.md cross-gate posture note is updated to name the newly enabled blocking criteria; verify by grep for the criteria routing keys in the updated paragraph.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan: specify the revert procedure (e.g., 'set blocking_enabled: false on the new criteria in criteria_routing.json and re-run tests'), the trigger condition (e.g., observed false-block rate > N in a rolling window), and who can authorize an emergency revert.
findings.finding: The plan makes a production behavioral breaking change (LLM criteria flip from advisory to blocking, affecting all PR authors) but provides no explicit rollback or back-out plan for post-deployment issues. The conditional escape ('this AC is renegotiated') applies only to the pre-deployment analysis phase; there is no described mechanism to revert if false-blocks emerge in production after the config ships.
findings.location: What section, AC4, Why section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection: 'If post-deployment false-block rate exceeds 0 in any 5-day window, revert criteria_routing.json to blocking_enabled: false on the newly enabled criteria (restore prior state), re-run make test, and open a follow-up to re-derive the threshold. The revert requires no ADR amendment — the ADR 0036 note should record the revert date if triggered.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC3 to require ADR 0036 to record the FINAL confirmed criterion→consequence-binary mapping (as verified against verifier prompts during implementation), not just a dated citation of 9f25 — so the ADR is the authoritative source for future blocking-set changes.
findings.finding: The criterion→consequence-binary mapping table is explicitly marked 'to be confirmed against the verifier prompts during implementation,' but AC3's ADR update requirement only mandates 'a dated enablement note citing derivation ticket 9f25.' If the mapping changes during implementation, there is no AC or Scope item requiring the ADR to record the FINAL confirmed mapping (which criteria block for which serious binaries and why). Future maintainers reading ADR 0036 may see only the derivation citation, not the authoritative mapping rationale.
findings.location: AC3 ('ADR 0036 carries a dated enablement note citing derivation ticket 9f25') and What section ('to be confirmed against the verifier prompts during implementation')
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC3 to: 'ADR 0036 carries a dated enablement note citing derivation ticket 9f25 AND documents the final confirmed mapping of serious consequence-binaries to owning routing-key criteria (as verified against the verifier prompts), so the ADR is the authoritative record for any future blocking-set evolution.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure (e.g., `git revert` + redeploy SLA) and a post-deploy monitoring threshold (e.g., 'revert if false-block rate > 0 in first N PRs') to the plan, and consider enabling criteria one at a time (or in two batches) to isolate any false-block to a specific criterion.
findings.finding: The plan flips blocking_enabled for ~5 criteria simultaneously in a single deployment with no staged per-criterion rollout, no post-deploy false-block monitoring threshold, and no explicitly documented rollback procedure; if a previously-unseen false-block appears in production after the flip, there is no articulated response path or revert trigger.
findings.location: ## What / Scope / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection under Scope: 'Revert: `git revert <sha>` + deploy; target < 15 min MTTR. Post-deploy: monitor false-block rate for the first 50 PRs; if > 0, revert all flipped criteria and renegotiate per-criterion staging.' Optionally split the flip into two PRs: {security, api-compat} first, then {db-migrations, deletion-impact, tests, scope-intent} after a 1-week observation window.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering the required update to docs/plan-review-gate.md (e.g. 'The cross-gate posture note in docs/plan-review-gate.md is updated to reflect that serious-tier criteria are now blocking at the derived threshold').
findings.finding: docs/plan-review-gate.md is listed in Scope as an in-scope artifact but no Acceptance Criterion covers its required update, leaving the end-state of that document unverifiable.
findings.location: Scope section (mentions docs/plan-review-gate.md); Acceptance Criteria section (no corresponding AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] docs/plan-review-gate.md cross-gate posture note is updated to record that serious-tier LLM criteria are now blocking at the derived threshold, consistent with ADR 0036 enablement note.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the Scope / What section to list all tests that hardcode pre-flip (0.95, False) values for the targeted criteria (test_threshold_for_default_overlay_is_advisory_at_095, test_threshold_for_takes_min_threshold_and_any_blocking, test_code_review_deletion_impact.py line 104, test_code_review_scope_intent.py line 75) and confirm each is updated to the new threshold/blocking values.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope names only 2 pinning tests to update (test_impact_model_versioning.py::test_code_review_only_det_security_criteria_are_hard_blocks and test_code_review_ws2.py::test_secrets_security_keys_are_the_ws5_blocking_handoff), but at least 4 additional tests in the existing suite hardcode the pre-flip (0.95, False) values for criteria the plan will flip, and will fail on 'make test': (1) test_code_review_ws2.py::test_threshold_for_default_overlay_is_advisory_at_095 asserts reg.threshold_for(['security']) == (0.95, False); (2) test_code_review_ws2.py::test_threshold_for_takes_min_threshold_and_any_blocking asserts reg.threshold_for(['security', 'performance']) == (0.95, False); (3) test_code_review_deletion_impact.py line 104 asserts reg.threshold_for(['deletion-impact']) == (0.95, False); (4) test_code_review_scope_intent.py line 75 asserts reg.threshold_for(['scope-intent']) == (0.95, False).
findings.location: Scope section; tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py lines 38-52; tests/unit/test_code_review_deletion_impact.py line 104; tests/unit/test_code_review_scope_intent.py line 75
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py::test_threshold_for_default_overlay_is_advisory_at_095 and test_threshold_for_takes_min_threshold_and_any_blocking (hardcode security at (0.95, False)), tests/unit/test_code_review_deletion_impact.py line 104, tests/unit/test_code_review_scope_intent.py line 75 — all must be updated to reflect the new (derived_threshold, True) values for their respective criteria.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'all 15 LLM criteria' count in the Why section to '13' (the actual number of OVERLAY_IDS entries in `criteria_routing.json`).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'all 15 LLM criteria ship blocking_enabled: false at 0.95', but reading `criteria_routing.json` and `OVERLAY_IDS` shows exactly 13 LLM overlay criteria (security, performance, i18n, a11y, db-migrations, docs, supply-chain, api-compat, iac, tests, llm-prompts, deletion-impact, scope-intent), not 15. The count is wrong by 2.
findings.location: Why section: 'all 15 LLM criteria ship blocking_enabled: false at 0.95'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '15 LLM criteria' with '13 LLM criteria' — the 13 entries in OVERLAY_IDS (security, performance, i18n, a11y, db-migrations, docs, supply-chain, api-compat, iac, tests, llm-prompts, deletion-impact, scope-intent).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_code_review_scope_intent.py` (line 74) and `tests/unit/test_code_review_deletion_impact.py` (line 102) to the Scope and update their `blocking_enabled is False` assertions to reflect the new approved blocking posture for scope-intent and deletion-impact.
findings.finding: Two overlay-specific test files outside the plan's named scope contain hard assertions that `blocking_enabled is False` on exactly the criteria the plan will flip to `true`. `tests/unit/test_code_review_scope_intent.py` line 74 asserts `entry['blocking_enabled'] is False` for `scope-intent`; `tests/unit/test_code_review_deletion_impact.py` line 102 asserts `entry['blocking_enabled'] is False` for `deletion-impact`. Both are in the plan's approved flip set (scope-intent and deletion-impact). The plan names only the two cross-story pinning tests as update targets and does not enumerate these per-overlay invariant tests, so they will break on the criteria_routing.json change without being updated.
findings.location: Scope section / tests/unit/test_code_review_scope_intent.py:74 / tests/unit/test_code_review_deletion_impact.py:102
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, append: `tests/unit/test_code_review_scope_intent.py` (update `blocking_enabled is False` → `True` for scope-intent), `tests/unit/test_code_review_deletion_impact.py` (same for deletion-impact). Or add a note that all per-overlay `blocking_enabled` invariant tests must be updated when a criterion is promoted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `code-review-deletion-impact.md` and `code-review-scope-intent.md` to remove or revise the 'This overlay is ADVISORY (coach-not-block)' instruction when those criteria gain `blocking_enabled: true`, replacing it with posture-neutral guidance (e.g. 'your findings flow through Pass-2 verify + Pass-3 decide').
findings.finding: The `deletion-impact` and `scope-intent` Pass-1 overlay prompts contain the explicit instruction 'This overlay is ADVISORY (coach-not-block)'. The plan enables `blocking_enabled: true` on both criteria, but these prompts will still tell the LLM it is running in an advisory-only capacity. Although the blocking decision is made in Pass-3 (not by the LLM), a Pass-1 LLM that believes its output is advisory may under-emit, soften, or hedge findings it would otherwise make firmly — suppressing the serious-tier findings that the new blocking posture depends on.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-deletion-impact.md (line 41) and src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-scope-intent.md (line 45–46); plan What section — criteria to enable blocking
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'This overlay is ADVISORY (coach-not-block); your claims flow through Pass-2 verify + Pass-3 decide, so ground every one.' with 'Your findings flow through Pass-2 verify + Pass-3 decide — ground every one.' in both affected prompts. Do not state the blocking posture in the Pass-1 prompt at all.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one integration/behavioral test that exercises the routing code end-to-end with a non-DET LLM criterion (e.g., `security` or `api-compat`) configured as blocking_enabled: true and a finding score above the derived threshold, asserting the routing layer returns an actual BLOCK decision — not just that the config key is set correctly.
findings.finding: The testing plan covers only config-pinning (blocking_enabled set on the right JSON keys) and offline score-replay (historical sidecar data), but provides no explicit integration/behavioral test that the routing code actually invokes the blocking decision for a non-DET LLM criterion at runtime. Since the plan states 'Today only the two DET criteria can block,' the routing code may contain a type-gated conditional (e.g., only DET entries trigger the blocking path) that would cause LLM criteria to remain advisory even after the config flip — and neither the pinning tests nor the offline replay would catch this regression.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1, AC2, AC4; What section (blocking_enabled flip on LLM criteria)
findings.suggested_fix: In tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py (or a new test_criteria_routing_blocking.py), add a parametrized test that instantiates the router with a mocked criteria_routing.json entry for a non-DET criterion (e.g., security) with blocking_enabled: true and block_threshold: 0.6, feeds a finding with score 0.75, and asserts the router's evaluate/route method returns a blocking outcome. This verifies the routing code reads blocking_enabled generically (not only for DET) and is the minimal behavioral assertion missing from the plan.

findings.finding: AC4 requires a corpus replay that shows #518-class and #539-class findings now BLOCK and the false-block count stays 0, but no proving command, script path, or corpus location is specified. The 'post-recal corpus' is undefined in this plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'A replay of the post-recal corpus shows #518-class and #539-class findings now BLOCK while the observed false-block count stays 0.'

findings.finding: AC item 4 ('A replay of the post-recal corpus shows #518-class and #539-class findings now BLOCK while the observed false-block count stays 0') names an end-state-observable outcome but does not specify how or where the replay is executed or where the evidence artifact is recorded/attached, leaving the acceptance criterion partially unverifiable without context from the sibling derivation story.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — item 4

findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section lists the five serious-tier severity-attribute binaries (data_loss_without_recovery, security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere, silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision, unversioned_published_contract_break, safety_net_removal_without_replacement) as if they are criterion IDs to set in criteria_routing.json, but they are verifier-model `severity_attributes` fields (defined in decide.py's `_CODE_PROD_BINARIES`/`_CODE_MAINT_BINARIES` and verify.py's `code_review_verification_model`), not routing keys. The routing.json is keyed on overlay IDs (`security`, `api-compat`, `iac`, etc.). The plan gives no mapping from these binary names to the overlay IDs that should get `blocking_enabled: true`.
findings.location: What section — 'set blocking_enabled: true + block_threshold = the derived value (~0.6, per the sibling story's crossover analysis) for the serious-tier lanes: security, and the criteria whose findings map to the serious prod/maint binaries (data_loss_without_recovery, ...)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table such as: `data_loss_without_recovery → [security, db-migrations, ...]`, `security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere → [security, iac, ...]`, etc., derived from the verifier rubric in `code-review-verify.md` and/or the existing labeled corpus. Alternatively, state the overlay IDs directly in the What section.

findings.finding: The plan names only `tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py` as the pinning test to update, but `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py::test_secrets_security_keys_are_the_ws5_blocking_handoff` (line 55) also asserts `set(blocking) == {'secret-detection', 'high-critical-security'}` and will fail if any additional criterion becomes blocking-enabled.
findings.location: What section — 'Update … the pinning test to the new approved blocking set'; Scope section — 'tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py` to the Scope list alongside `tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py`, and extend the What bullet to read: 'Update the pinning tests in `test_impact_model_versioning.py` AND `test_code_review_ws2.py::test_secrets_security_keys_are_the_ws5_blocking_handoff` to the new approved blocking set.'

findings.finding: The plan names five consequence binaries (data_loss_without_recovery, security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere, silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision, unversioned_published_contract_break, safety_net_removal_without_replacement) as criteria to flip `blocking_enabled: true` in `criteria_routing.json`, but these are NOT keys in `criteria_routing.json` — they are verify.py attribute fields and decide.py map entries. The actual `criteria_routing.json` keys for LLM overlays are overlay IDs (security, performance, db-migrations, etc.). The plan conflates the consequence-binary layer with the overlay-routing-key layer, leaving the executor with no clear mapping.
findings.location: What section: 'set blocking_enabled: true + block_threshold = the derived value (~0.6, per the sibling story's crossover analysis) for the serious-tier lanes: security, and the criteria whose findings map to the serious prod/maint binaries (data_loss_without_recovery, security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere, silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision, unversioned_published_contract_break, safety_net_removal_without_replacement)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the consequence-binary list with the actual overlay IDs that should become blocking (e.g., `security` and any others whose findings commonly surface serious-tier binaries), or add a table mapping each overlay ID to its serious-tier risk rationale.

findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py::test_secrets_security_keys_are_the_ws5_blocking_handoff` at line 64 asserts `set(blocking) == {'secret-detection', 'high-critical-security'}` (the exact same invariant as the named pinning test). Adding any LLM criterion to the blocking set will break this test, but the plan does not enumerate it as a test to update.
findings.location: Scope section: 'tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py` to the Scope list and note that `test_secrets_security_keys_are_the_ws5_blocking_handoff` must be updated to the new approved blocking set.

findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section names consequence-binary fields (`data_loss_without_recovery`, `security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere`, `silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision`, `unversioned_published_contract_break`, `safety_net_removal_without_replacement`) as the routing keys to flip in `criteria_routing.json`, but these names do NOT exist as overlay routing keys in that file. They are `CodeSeverityAttrs` verification-attribute fields in `verify.py` and tier-tagged entries in `decide.py`'s `_CODE_PROD_BINARIES`/`_CODE_MAINT_BINARIES`. The actual routing keys are overlay ids (`"security"`, `"api-compat"`, `"tests"`, etc.). Because a single overlay finding can set any combination of consequence binaries (e.g. `data_loss_without_recovery` can fire from `db-migrations`, `iac`, `security`, or `api-compat`), the plan does not unambiguously specify WHICH overlay routing keys should receive `blocking_enabled: true` — leaving the implementer to guess the intended mapping and risking either under-blocking (wrong overlay omitted) or over-blocking (a non-serious overlay flipped).
findings.location: ## What — second bullet: '...blocking_enabled: true + block_threshold = the derived value (~0.6, per the sibling story's crossover analysis) for the serious-tier lanes: security, and the criteria whose findings map to the serious prod/maint binaries (data_loss_without_recovery, security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere, silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision, unversioned_published_contract_break, safety_net_removal_without_replacement)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, replace the consequence-binary list with the explicit overlay routing-key list (e.g. 'set blocking_enabled: true on the `security` overlay; all other overlays remain advisory at 0.95'). If the intent is to block only when a *specific* binary fires, note that the current threshold_for() mechanism resolves blocking at the overlay level, not the binary level — and specify whether a new per-binary routing mechanism is needed or whether the overlay-level flip is sufficient.

findings.finding: The plan names five consequence-binary verifier-attribute fields ('data_loss_without_recovery', 'security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere', 'silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision', 'unversioned_published_contract_break', 'safety_net_removal_without_replacement') as the 'serious-tier criteria' to flip to blocking_enabled:true in criteria_routing.json, but these are CodeSeverityAttrs fields in the verifier schema (verify.py / decide.py), not criterion routing keys. criteria_routing.json is keyed by overlay/dimension IDs ('security', 'api-compat', 'tests', etc.). No mapping from these binary names to the actual routing criterion IDs that must receive blocking_enabled:true is stated. An executor cannot determine which JSON keys in criteria_routing.json to update, and the pinning test operates on routing criterion IDs, not binary names.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section; src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json; src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py lines 325-365; src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py lines 191-205
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, replace or augment the binary-name list with the actual routing criterion IDs, e.g.: 'Set blocking_enabled: true + block_threshold = <derived> for: security, api-compat (catches unversioned_published_contract_break), tests (catches safety_net_removal_without_replacement)' — plus confirm whether a single overlay key covers all three prod binaries or whether they span multiple criteria.

findings.finding: AC3 requires ADR 0036 to cite 'the derivation ticket' but the ticket identifier is not named anywhere in the plan. An executor writing the ADR note does not know which ticket to cite.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'ADR 0036 carries a dated enablement note citing the derivation ticket.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
